era_context: The year 1720 falls in the early eighteenth century, an era of great-power consolidation and spectacular financial experimentation. The Great Northern War (1700–1721) was winding down, with Sweden losing its Baltic empire to a resurgent Russia under Peter I. In western Europe, Britain and France were both convulsed by unprecedented speculative bubbles — the South Sea Company and the Mississippi Company respectively — whose near-simultaneous collapses reshaped public finance, regulatory policy, and political careers for decades. The Qing dynasty under the Kangxi Emperor was intervening militarily in Tibet to expel Dzungar Mongol forces, extending Qing influence deep into Inner Asia. The Mughal Empire continued its post-Aurangzeb fragmentation under a rapid succession of weak emperors. In the Americas, European colonial expansion continued, while the tail end of the 'Golden Age of Piracy' saw dramatic captures and executions.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading posts and Ethiopian chronicles
Interior South America beyond Iberian colonial records
Aboriginal Australia and interior Pacific Islands
Central Asian polities between Russian and Qing spheres
Interior Southeast Asia (Cambodia, Laos, highland Burma)

events.title: South Sea Bubble collapses in Britain
events.description: The South Sea Company, which had assumed a large portion of the British national debt in exchange for exclusive trading rights and stock conversion privileges, saw its share price surge from roughly £128 in January to over £1,000 by late June before collapsing catastrophically through August–September, falling below £200 by December. The speculative mania had drawn in investors from all social classes, including members of Parliament and the royal household. The crash ruined thousands of investors, triggered a political crisis that brought down several ministers and directors (the 'Screen' inquiry), and led to lasting changes in British financial regulation. Robert Walpole's management of the aftermath propelled him to effective control of government, laying the foundations for his long ministry often described as Britain's first prime ministership.
key_figures: Robert Walpole (Whig statesman, managed the crisis)
John Aislabie (Chancellor of the Exchequer, later expelled from Parliament)
Charles Stanhope (Secretary of the Treasury, acquitted)

sources.name: John Carswell, The South Sea Bubble (1960; revised 1993)
House of Commons journals and committee reports on the South Sea affair (1720–1721)
Richard Dale, The First Crash: Lessons from the South Sea Bubble (2004)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in parliamentary records, company accounts, contemporary pamphlets, and correspondence. Share prices are traceable in contemporary periodicals.

events.title: Collapse of the Mississippi Bubble in France
events.description: John Law's Compagnie du Mississippi (Mississippi Company), which had effectively absorbed French public debt and the national bank, saw its share price collapse beginning in May 1720 after the Regent's government attempted a phased devaluation of both shares and banknotes. Panic ensued: Law was dismissed as Controller-General of Finances in May, the rue Quincampoix exchange was shut down, and crowds besieged the Banque Royale demanding coin for paper. By late 1720, Law's entire 'System' — which had attempted to monetise colonial assets and replace metallic currency with paper — was dismantled. Law himself fled France in December. The episode left France deeply distrustful of paper money and central banking for over a century, contributing to the fiscal rigidity that would plague the ancien régime until 1789.
key_figures: John Law (Scottish financier, Controller-General of Finances)
Philippe II, Duke of Orléans (Regent of France)
Marc-René de Voyer de Paulmy d'Argenson (Keeper of the Seals)

sources.name: Antoin Murphy, John Law: Economic Theorist and Policy-Maker (1997)
Duc de Saint-Simon, Mémoires (composed c. 1739–1750, covering 1694–1723)
Arrêts du Conseil d'État du roi (royal council decrees, 1720)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in French government decrees, contemporary gazettes, diplomatic correspondence, and later memoirs.

events.title: Bubble Act passed by British Parliament
events.description: In June 1720, Parliament passed the Royal Exchange and London Assurance Corporation Act (6 Geo I, c. 18), commonly known as the Bubble Act, which prohibited the formation of joint-stock companies without a royal charter or act of Parliament. Ironically, the Act was promoted in part by South Sea Company insiders seeking to suppress rival speculative schemes that were drawing capital away from South Sea stock. While intended to protect the South Sea Company's position, the Act long outlived the bubble itself and remained on the statute books until 1825, significantly constraining British corporate development for over a century.
key_figures: South Sea Company directors (lobbied for the Act)

sources.name: 6 Geo I, c. 18 — Royal Exchange and London Assurance Corporation Act (1720)
Ron Harris, Industrializing English Law: Entrepreneurship and Business Organization, 1720–1844 (2000)

events.certainty_note: The statute survives and its passage date is recorded in parliamentary journals.

events.title: Great Plague of Marseille begins
events.description: In late May 1720, plague broke out in the port city of Marseille, likely introduced via the merchant ship Grand-Saint-Antoine arriving from the Levant. Despite quarantine protocols, infected goods were released into the city. The epidemic ravaged Marseille through 1720–1722, killing an estimated 50,000 of the city's roughly 90,000 inhabitants, with the broader Provence region losing approximately 100,000 people total. It was the last major outbreak of bubonic plague in western Europe. The city's commerce was devastated, and the French government imposed a rigorous cordon sanitaire (the Mur de la Peste) extending across Provence. The disaster prompted lasting reforms in French quarantine and public health procedures.
key_figures: Chevalier Nicolas Roze (commanded plague burial details)
Monseigneur de Belsunce (Bishop of Marseille, remained to minister to the sick)

sources.name: Jean-Baptiste Bertrand, Relation historique de la peste de Marseille en 1720 (1721)
Daniel Panzac, Quarantaines et lazarets: l'Europe et la peste d'Orient (1986)
Municipal archives of Marseille, deliberations of the échevins (1720)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by contemporary municipal records, medical reports, and narratives. Death toll estimates vary between sources but the order of magnitude is not disputed.

events.title: Treaty of The Hague ends the War of the Quadruple Alliance
events.description: On 17 February 1720, Spain acceded to the Treaty of The Hague, effectively ending the War of the Quadruple Alliance (1718–1720). Spain's chief minister Cardinal Giulio Alberoni had been dismissed in December 1719, removing the principal architect of Spanish revisionism. Under the treaty, Philip V of Spain renounced claims to territories in Italy gained by Austria at Utrecht, while receiving a promise that the Duchy of Parma would eventually pass to a Spanish Bourbon prince. Savoy exchanged Sicily for Sardinia with Austria. The settlement confirmed the post-Utrecht European order and temporarily checked Spanish attempts to revise the 1713 settlement.
key_figures: Philippe II, Duke of Orléans (Regent of France)

sources.name: Treaty text, The Hague, 17 February 1720
Henry Kamen, Philip V of Spain: The King Who Reigned Twice (2001)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives; date firmly established in diplomatic records.

events.title: Frederick I ascends the Swedish throne; new constitution inaugurates the Age of Liberty
events.description: On 29 February 1720 (O.S.; 11 March N.S.), Queen Ulrika Eleonora abdicated the Swedish throne in favour of her husband Frederick of Hesse, who was elected king by the Riksdag as Frederick I. The transfer of power was conditional upon Frederick accepting a new constitution that drastically curtailed royal authority and vested executive power in the Riksråd (Council of the Realm) and legislative supremacy in the Riksdag. This constitutional revolution, which followed Charles XII's death in 1718 and the collapse of Swedish absolutism, inaugurated Sweden's 'Age of Liberty' (Frihetstiden, 1719–1772), a parliamentary-dominated era unique in contemporary Europe.
key_figures: Frederick I (King of Sweden, formerly Frederick of Hesse-Kassel)
Arvid Horn (President of the Chancellery, dominant political figure)

sources.name: Swedish Riksdag records and Regeringsformen of 1720
Michael Roberts, The Age of Liberty: Sweden 1719–1772 (1986)

events.certainty_note: Constitutional text and Riksdag proceedings survive. Dates are firm in Swedish administrative records.

events.title: Treaty of Frederiksborg between Denmark-Norway and Sweden
events.description: On 3 July 1720, Denmark-Norway and Sweden signed the Treaty of Frederiksborg, ending hostilities between the two kingdoms in the Great Northern War. Sweden ceded its exemption from the Sound Dues and abandoned its alliance with the Duke of Holstein-Gottorp, paying Denmark 600,000 riksdaler. Denmark returned its conquests in Swedish Pomerania and Bremen-Verden but consolidated its position as the dominant Scandinavian power. The treaty was one of several bilateral peace settlements that progressively wound down the Great Northern War before the final Russo-Swedish Treaty of Nystad in 1721.

sources.name: Treaty of Frederiksborg text (3 July 1720)
Stewart Oakley, War and Peace in the Baltic, 1560–1790 (1992)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives in Danish and Swedish archives. Date is unambiguous.

events.title: Battle of Grengam — Russian naval victory over Sweden
events.description: On 7 August 1720 (27 July O.S.), a Russian galley fleet under General Mikhail Golitsyn engaged and defeated a Swedish sailing squadron near the island of Grengam (Rilax) in the Åland archipelago. The Russians captured four Swedish frigates despite being outgunned, exploiting shallow coastal waters where Swedish heavy vessels could not manoeuvre. Grengam was the last significant naval battle of the Great Northern War and demonstrated the maturity of the Russian navy that Peter I had built virtually from nothing since the 1690s. The victory strengthened Russia's negotiating position in the peace talks that culminated in the Treaty of Nystad the following year.
key_figures: Mikhail Golitsyn (Russian general commanding the galley fleet)

sources.name: Russian Admiralty reports and Peter I's correspondence (1720)
Robert K. Massie, Peter the Great: His Life and World (1980)

events.certainty_note: Documented in both Russian and Swedish naval records. Date and outcome are not disputed.

events.title: Qing expedition expels Dzungar forces from Tibet
events.description: In 1720, a Qing military expedition under the command of Yanxin and Galbi entered Tibet and expelled the Dzungar Mongol occupation force that had controlled Lhasa since 1717. The Dzungars had killed the pretender Lha-bzang Khan and terrorised the monasteries. The Qing army installed the young Kelzang Gyatso as the seventh Dalai Lama and established a permanent Qing military garrison and resident (amban) in Lhasa for the first time. This campaign marked a decisive extension of Qing authority over Tibet, transforming it from a nominal tributary relationship into direct political supervision. It also represented a major setback for the Dzungar Khanate's bid to dominate Inner Asian Buddhist politics.
key_figures: Kangxi Emperor (Qing ruler who ordered the campaign)

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty), Kangxi reign
Peter Perdue, China Marches West: The Qing Conquest of Central Eurasia (2005)
Luciano Petech, China and Tibet in the Early XVIIIth Century (1950; 2nd ed. 1972)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Qing official records, Tibetan chronicles, and Jesuit missionary correspondence. The campaign's year is firmly established; some internal chronology of specific engagements varies by source.

events.title: Declaratory Act asserts British parliamentary supremacy over Ireland
events.description: The British Parliament passed the Dependency of Ireland on Great Britain Act 1719 (effective 1720), commonly known as the Declaratory Act (6 Geo I, c. 5), asserting that the British Parliament had the right to legislate for Ireland and that the Irish House of Lords had no appellate jurisdiction. The Act was prompted by the Annesley v. Sherlock case, in which the Irish and British Lords had issued conflicting rulings. It reduced the Irish Parliament to a subordinate body and became a lasting source of Irish constitutional grievance, remaining in force until its repeal in 1782 during the movement for 'Grattan's Parliament.' The principle it embodied — metropolitan legislative supremacy over a dependent kingdom — was later echoed in the 1766 Declaratory Act applied to the American colonies.
key_figures: George I (King of Great Britain and Ireland)
William Conolly (Speaker of the Irish House of Commons)

sources.name: 6 Geo I, c. 5 — Dependency of Ireland on Great Britain Act (1719)
James Kelly, Poynings' Law and the Making of Law in Ireland, 1660–1800 (2007)

events.certainty_note: Statute text survives. The Act was passed in 1719 but its practical enforcement and political consequences unfolded from 1720 onward.

events.title: Capture of Calico Jack Rackham, Anne Bonny, and Mary Read
events.description: In October–November 1720, the pirate captain John 'Calico Jack' Rackham and his crew — notably including Anne Bonny and Mary Read, two of the most famous female pirates in recorded history — were captured by a privateer sloop under Jonathan Barnet off the western coast of Jamaica. Rackham was tried and hanged at Port Royal on 18 November 1720. Bonny and Read were convicted but received stays of execution due to pregnancy. Their capture was part of the broader British suppression campaign against Caribbean piracy following the expiration of the 1718 amnesty. The episode became iconic in pirate lore and gender history, though later romanticisation has often obscured the violent reality of their careers.
key_figures: Jonathan Barnet (privateer who captured the crew)

sources.name: Captain Charles Johnson (possibly Daniel Defoe), A General History of the Pyrates (1724)
Trial records, Vice-Admiralty Court of Jamaica (November 1720)
David Cordingly, Under the Black Flag: The Romance and Reality of Life Among the Pirates (1995)

events.certainty_note: Trial records survive in Jamaican colonial archives. Dates of capture and execution are documented in official correspondence with London.

events.title: Muhammad Shah consolidates Mughal rule
events.description: Muhammad Shah, who had been placed on the Mughal throne in September 1719 by the Sayyid Brothers (Husain Ali Khan and Abdullah Khan), began consolidating his authority in 1720 when the Sayyid Brothers were overthrown. Husain Ali Khan was assassinated in October 1720 during a military campaign in the Deccan, and Abdullah Khan was subsequently defeated and imprisoned. Muhammad Shah then ruled in conjunction with his chief minister Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah I, though the emperor's own passivity and the growing autonomy of provincial governors meant that effective Mughal central authority continued to decline. The removal of the Sayyid 'king-makers' marked the end of the most chaotic phase of post-Aurangzeb succession struggles but did not arrest the empire's fragmentation.
key_figures: Abdullah Khan (Sayyid Brother, defeated and imprisoned)
Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah I (rising chief minister)

sources.name: Khafi Khan, Muntakhab al-Lubab (chronicle of Mughal India, composed c. 1720s)
William Irvine, Later Mughals (1922; reprint 1971)
Satish Chandra, Parties and Politics at the Mughal Court, 1707–1740 (1959; 4th ed. 2002)

events.certainty_note: Contemporary Mughal chronicles and European (Dutch, English) factory records document these events in detail, though exact dates sometimes vary between sources.

events.title: Kingdom of Dahomey conquers the Kingdom of Allada
events.description: Around 1720, the inland Kingdom of Dahomey under King Agaja began its military expansion toward the coast by conquering the older Kingdom of Allada (Ardra). This conquest gave Dahomey access to the Atlantic slave trade ports and marked the beginning of Dahomey's transformation from an interior tributary state into one of the most powerful and centralised kingdoms in West Africa. The precise year is debated — some sources place the conquest of Allada in 1724 — but oral traditions and European trading post records indicate that aggressive Dahomean military campaigns against coastal states were underway by 1720. Dahomey's rise would reshape the political economy of the Slave Coast for the next century and a half.

sources.name: Robin Law, The Slave Coast of West Africa, 1550–1750 (1991)
European (primarily English and French) trading post correspondence from Ouidah and Allada, 1710s–1720s
Edna Bay, Wives of the Leopard: Gender, Politics, and Culture in the Kingdom of Dahomey (1998)

events.certainty_note: The dating of Dahomey's early campaigns is contested. European records confirm escalating warfare in the 1720s, but whether the fall of Allada was 1720, 1724, or a process spanning those years depends on the source tradition. Oral histories and European records diverge on specific years.

events.title: Spanish missions established in East Texas
events.description: In 1720, the Marqués de San Miguel de Aguayo led a major Spanish expedition to re-establish missions and presidios in East Texas that had been abandoned during the French-Spanish tensions of 1719 (the 'Chicken War'). Spain feared French encroachment from Louisiana, and the Aguayo expedition re-founded or reinforced approximately ten missions and four presidios, including the establishment of a permanent Spanish presence at Los Adaes near the French post at Natchitoches. This effort solidified Spanish territorial claims in Texas for the next century, though actual colonial settlement remained sparse and heavily dependent on indigenous cooperation.
key_figures: Marqués de San Miguel de Aguayo (Governor of Coahuila and Texas)
Louis Juchereau de Saint-Denis (French commander at Natchitoches)

sources.name: Aguayo expedition diary and reports (1720–1721), in Archivo General de la Nación, Mexico
Donald Chipman, Spanish Texas, 1519–1821 (1992)

events.certainty_note: Expedition records, mission registers, and colonial correspondence survive in Mexican and Spanish archives.

events.title: Battle of the Villasur Expedition on the Great Plains
events.description: In August 1720, a Spanish military expedition of approximately 45 soldiers, 60 Pueblo Indian auxiliaries, and a chaplain under Lieutenant General Pedro de Villasur was ambushed and virtually annihilated by Pawnee and Otoe warriors, possibly assisted by French traders, near the confluence of the Loup and Platte Rivers in present-day Nebraska. Villasur and about 35 Spaniards were killed. The disaster was the worst Spanish military defeat on the Great Plains and effectively halted Spanish northward expansion beyond New Mexico. It demonstrated the limits of Spanish power in the continental interior and the growing influence of French-allied indigenous confederacies.
key_figures: Pedro de Villasur (Spanish lieutenant general, killed)
Antonio Valverde y Cosío (Governor of New Mexico, ordered the expedition)
Jean de l'Archevêque (French-born scout, killed in the battle)

sources.name: Segesser hide paintings (c. 1720s, depict the battle; Palace of the Governors, Santa Fe)
Alfred Barnaby Thomas, After Coronado: Spanish Exploration Northeast of New Mexico, 1696–1727 (1935)
Spanish colonial correspondence, Archivo General de Indias

events.certainty_note: Spanish colonial reports and the unique Segesser hide paintings provide strong evidence. Exact date in August is uncertain (sources suggest 13–14 August), and the degree of French involvement is debated.

events.title: Treaty of Stockholm between Sweden and Prussia
events.description: On 1 February 1720, Sweden and Prussia signed the Treaty of Stockholm, by which Sweden ceded the southern portion of Swedish Pomerania (including Stettin and the Oder mouth) to Prussia in exchange for two million thalers. This was one of several bilateral treaties by which Sweden progressively liquidated its German territories as the Great Northern War wound down. For Prussia, the acquisition of Stettin and the Oder estuary was a significant step in its rise as a north German power, providing commercial access and strategic depth that the Hohenzollerns had long coveted.

sources.name: Treaty of Stockholm text (1 February 1720)
Christopher Clark, Iron Kingdom: The Rise and Downfall of Prussia, 1600–1947 (2006)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives in Prussian and Swedish archives.

events.title: Safavid Persia faces Afghan tribal uprisings
events.description: By 1720, the Safavid Empire under Sultan Husayn faced escalating Afghan tribal revolts in its eastern provinces. Mir Mahmud Hotaki, who had succeeded his father Mir Wais as leader of the Ghilzai Afghans in Kandahar, was consolidating power and preparing for the invasion that would culminate in the fall of Isfahan in 1722. Simultaneously, Abdalis around Herat were in intermittent revolt. The Safavid court, weakened by decades of administrative decline, clerical interference, and military neglect, proved incapable of mounting effective counter-campaigns. These uprisings marked the terminal phase of the Safavid dynasty, one of the most consequential state collapses in early modern Middle Eastern history.

sources.name: Judasz Tadeusz Krusinski, The History of the Late Revolutions of Persia (English trans. 1740, from observations c. 1720–1725)
Rudi Matthee, Persia in Crisis: Safavid Decline and the Fall of Isfahan (2012)

events.certainty_note: European missionary and diplomatic accounts provide the best contemporary documentation. Persian court chronicles from this period are sparse, and the precise chronology of 1720 events in the eastern provinces is sometimes unclear.

events.title: Edo period Japan: Tokugawa Yoshimune's Kyōhō Reforms underway
events.description: Tokugawa Yoshimune, who became the eighth shōgun in 1716, continued his programme of fiscal and administrative reforms known as the Kyōhō Reforms (享保の改革) through 1720. Key measures included encouraging new rice cultivation, regulating commodity prices, and reforming the shogunal bureaucracy. In 1720, Yoshimune relaxed the ban on the import of foreign (non-religious) books, particularly Chinese translations of Western scientific works, which opened a channel for Western learning (rangaku) that would prove culturally significant in later decades. This partial lifting of intellectual restrictions was a notable departure from the isolationist tendencies of the Tokugawa policy.

sources.name: Tokugawa jikki (Veritable Records of the Tokugawa), compiled by the Hayashi family
Harold Bolitho, 'The Tempō Crisis', in The Cambridge History of Japan, vol. 4 (1991); also Tsuji Tatsuya, 'Politics in the Eighteenth Century'

events.certainty_note: Shogunal edicts survive in Tokugawa administrative records. The relaxation of the book import ban is well attested but its exact scope in 1720 versus later years is debated.

events.title: Construction of the Karlskirche begins in Vienna
events.description: Construction of the Karlskirche (Church of St. Charles Borromeo) in Vienna began in 1716 and progressed significantly by 1720 under the design of Johann Bernhard Fischer von Erlach. Commissioned by Emperor Charles VI as a votive offering after the plague of 1713, the Karlskirche is considered one of the supreme achievements of Baroque architecture, blending Roman, Greek, and even Ottoman visual references in its facade. Fischer von Erlach died in 1723 and his son Joseph Emanuel completed the structure in 1737. The building remains an iconic landmark of Habsburg Vienna and a masterwork of early eighteenth-century architecture.

sources.name: Johann Bernhard Fischer von Erlach, Entwurff einer historischen Architectur (1721)
Hans Aurenhammer, J.B. Fischer von Erlach (1973)

events.certainty_note: Construction records and Fischer von Erlach's own publications document the project. 1720 is part of a multi-year construction process rather than a single dated event.

events.title: ండi Chinese encyclopedia project: Kangxi dictionary nearing completion
events.description: The Kangxi Zidian (康熙字典), the comprehensive Chinese character dictionary commissioned by the Kangxi Emperor, was being compiled under the editorship of Zhang Yushu and Chen Tingjing during this period, reaching completion in 1716. By 1720 it was in wide circulation and had become the authoritative standard reference for Chinese characters, listing 47,035 characters organized by 214 radicals. While the dictionary was technically completed before 1720, its dissemination and adoption as the standard lexicographic reference across the Qing empire was an ongoing process in this period. Its system of 214 radicals remained standard into the twentieth century.

sources.name: Kangxi Zidian (康熙字典), published 1716
S. Robert Ramsey, The Languages of China (1987)

events.certainty_note: The dictionary itself is extant. The date of completion (1716) is firm; 1720 represents its period of established authority rather than a specific dated event.

disconfirming_evidence: Several qualifications should be noted. (1) The dating of Dahomey's conquest of Allada (1720-013) is contested: Robin Law and other specialists have argued the definitive conquest occurred in 1724, not 1720, and that earlier accounts conflate preliminary raiding with the final campaign. Placing this event in 1720 follows older traditions that may be inaccurate. (2) The South Sea Bubble's causes are debated: some economic historians (e.g., Julian Hoppit) argue that the crash was less catastrophic for the broader economy than traditional narratives suggest, and that the political crisis was disproportionate to the actual financial damage outside of concentrated speculative circles. (3) The degree of French involvement in the Villasur defeat (1720-015) remains contested: Spanish officials claimed French instigation to justify their intelligence failures, but archaeological and documentary evidence is ambiguous about whether French traders actively participated in the ambush or merely traded with the Pawnee. (4) The characterisation of the Kangxi dictionary's 'dissemination' in 1720 (1720-020) is somewhat forced — the dictionary was completed in 1716, and no specific 1720 dissemination event is attested. Its inclusion reflects the ongoing cultural significance of the project rather than a discrete 1720 event. (5) Yoshimune's relaxation of the foreign book ban (1720-018) is sometimes dated to 1720 and sometimes to 1721 or 1722 depending on the source; the edict's scope was narrower than later rangaku enthusiasts retrospectively claimed.
historiographic_note: The year 1720 is extremely well documented for Europe owing to the dual financial crises (South Sea and Mississippi bubbles), which generated enormous volumes of parliamentary records, pamphlet literature, diplomatic correspondence, and subsequently became foundational case studies in economic history. The Great Northern War's winding-down is similarly well attested in Scandinavian, Russian, and German archives. For Asia, Qing records (Shilu, palace memorials) are substantial though the Tibetan campaign's documentation is filtered through both Qing imperial framing and later Tibetan retrospective accounts. For South Asia, Mughal chronicles exist but are less systematic for this chaotic period than for earlier reigns. West African events depend heavily on European trading post records, which are geographically narrow and commercially biased. The Americas are documented primarily through Spanish colonial archives, with indigenous perspectives recoverable only through archaeology and later oral traditions. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Oceanian events remain poorly attested in sources accessible to Western historiography.

graph_edges.note: The South Sea and Mississippi bubbles were interconnected speculative manias occurring simultaneously, with capital flows between them and shared investor psychology.
The Bubble Act was passed during the South Sea mania, partly at the South Sea Company's lobbying, to suppress rival schemes.
The Treaty of The Hague confirmed the post-Utrecht settlement that Spain had attempted to revise.
Charles XII's death in 1718 ended Swedish absolutism and led to the constitutional transformation of 1719–1720.
The Danish-Swedish peace was a precursor to the final Russo-Swedish Treaty of Nystad in 1721.
Russian naval victory at Grengam strengthened Russia's negotiating position for the Treaty of Nystad.
The Qing expedition responded to the Dzungar invasion and occupation of Tibet in 1717.
The Irish Declaratory Act of 1720 served as a precedent for the American Declaratory Act of 1766.
Both treaties were part of Sweden's liquidation of its overseas empire as the Great Northern War ended.
The Afghan uprisings of 1720 were the immediate prelude to Mir Mahmud's invasion and the fall of Isfahan in 1722.
The Mississippi Bubble's legacy of distrust toward paper money and banking contributed to the fiscal rigidity of the ancien régime that was a structural cause of the French Revolution.
